posts.name: J. Migone
posts.cooked:
Sooooo McArthur Bev had a store tasting in which they were serving Pape Clement. It had some interesting light tobacco, woodsy notes and a very nice mouthfeel, but overall it lacked flavor, seemed weak and was overall unremarkable. Possibly lacking structure? There were some back end tannins, but they seemed as subdued as the flavors. Compared to the much cheaper 2010 C. La Tour Carnet, this was underwhelming. To me this is a big “F” as I saw no circumstances in which I would enjoy this wine. Is this just me? I did not see the prices until after I made my decision.

In contrast, I had to taste the La Tour Carnet twice because I enjoyed it so much. Bought a bottle and need to buy more because I drank it already.

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked: Modern Pape Clement is an oak beverage towards which I often direct my Two Minutes Hate.

posts.name: Cr@!g Po++$
posts.cooked: I have not had either wine you mentioned, so my comment is based purely on prior experience with both producers, but it seems to me that your experience is in perfect harmony with what the wines are intended to deliver. The La Tour Carnet can never deliver what the Pape Clement is capable of in 20+ years, and likewise the latter is not intended to be nearly as serviceable as the former at age four. Hence the price differential.

posts.name: J. Migone
posts.cooked: Maybe, but I’ve had wines that deliver now and are expected to deliver in 20+ years. My opinion is that based on how thin and light this wine truly is, as well as the lack of backbone, it will not age well. It lacks everything I expect from a wine that can age.

posts.name: Max Kelly
posts.cooked:
I tasted the same line up. Didn’t think the Pape Clement was thin or light - seemed super-sleek, concentrated, very closed, and weighed down with a forest worth of oak. Certainly closed for business, but no discernible Bordeaux character. The PC wasn’t just young and tight - it was anonymous.

I thought the La Tour Carnet and Fombrauge both outclassed the PC by a mile for far less money. Surprised me since I generally like Pessac Leognan over Right Bank wines.

posts.name: J. Migone
posts.cooked:
Woohoo, somebody else was there! I’m still deciding about the Fombrauge…lol. The Merlot did come through as much as expected, but I got a bottle to try out in a few years.

I like the “anonymous” descriptor for the Clement (I’m borrowing it), along with unremarkable. The only thing it had going for it was that super sleek mouthfeel.

posts.name: R0bërt Gilb@ne
posts.cooked: Sounds like a lovely wine to avoid.

Bought a case of TM 2012, but not tasted yet… I’m a big fan of the domain and the best vintages to my taste are the Troplong Mondot 2009 (Very ripe and modern) and Troplong Mondot 2010 (much more classical, hint of green, and colder style vintage).

Of course it is a very concentrated wine, that will need a few years to calm down… but everything is there to make a great wine in 15 to 20 years

J. Migone:

La Tour Carnet 2010 is a great wine and ready to drink now… But put it in front of Troplong Mondot 2010… and you will understand the classification

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
Well – I will not open any good 2010 Bordeaux until the wines hit their 10th Birthday. At a minimum. This is one of the finest and most structured vintages of the last 50 years. The beauty of Bordeaux is the complexity the wines develop with age. Why some people taste a 2010 and a 2012 now and make statements about their inner quality is beyond me. It´s cleat that one will taste tons of fruit as long the wines are not closed and tons of wood when the wines are in their dormant phase. But it´s rare when a Bordeaux shows upfront oak when it is mature – so let´s say 15 or 20 years old. For me and many other Bordeaux lover the wines are of little interest when they are babies or teenagers.

It is interesting to taste a young Bordeaux here and there for science. But one needs tons of talent and experience to make a forecast with substance.

posts.name: R0bërt Gilb@ne
posts.cooked:
PhilippeCamps:

Bought a case of TM 2012, but not tasted yet… I’m a big fan of the domain and the best vintages to my taste are the Troplong Mondot 2009 (Very ripe and modern) and Troplong Mondot 2010 (much more classical, hint of green, and colder style vintage).

Of course it is a very concentrated wine, that will need a few years to calm down… but everything is there to make a great wine in 15 to 20 years

Phillippe, what vintages of Troplong Mondot have you had? I’m curious to know if you’ve had any older vintages but prefer newer ones. Obviously a fairly large shift stylistically between the two.

posts.name: Max Kelly
posts.cooked:
Jürgen Steinke:

Why some people taste a 2010 and a 2012 now and make statements about their inner quality is beyond me. It´s cleat that one will taste tons of fruit as long the wines are not closed and tons of wood when the wines are in their dormant phase. But it´s rare when a Bordeaux shows upfront oak when it is mature – so let´s say 15 or 20 years old. For me and many other Bordeaux lover the wines are of little interest when they are babies or teenagers.

It is interesting to taste a young Bordeaux here and there for science. But one needs tons of talent and experience to make a forecast with substance.

I think you’ve missed some important context here. The wines we’re discussing were offered by a representative of Bernard Magrez at a free tasting in a local wine shop. In other words, it was the wine-maker’s decision to show them off, presumably because he thinks there’s something to show. Nor was anyone questioning the quality of the vintages - just a single wine.

And while no one here is claiming to have the tasting chops of a pro, I think it’s entirely possible for educated consumers to make reasonably informed judgements about young wine, depending on the phase the wine is going through. Certainly there are dumb phases where I simply have no idea what will eventually emerge, but those are much more severe for Burgundy and Piedmont than BDX. Others on this board have far more experience, but I’ve tasted enough young BDX to stand by my judgment that the '12 PC is not to my taste. If it suits your palate, more power to you - I’ll just mourn the waste of good terroir.

posts.name: R0bërt Gilb@ne
posts.cooked:
Jürgen Steinke:

It is interesting to taste a young Bordeaux here and there for science. But one needs tons of talent and experience to make a forecast with substance.

When I drink in the name of science, The forecast is always good times ahead. My wife would most likely dispute the accuracy of this statement though.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
PhilippeCamps:

Bought a case of TM 2012, but not tasted yet… I’m a big fan of the domain and the best vintages to my taste are the Troplong Mondot 2009 (Very ripe and modern) and Troplong Mondot 2010 (much more classical, hint of green, and colder style vintage).

Of course it is a very concentrated wine, that will need a few years to calm down… but everything is there to make a great wine in 15 to 20 years

Calling the Troplong Mondot 2010 classical in any sense, even relative to the 09, is a pretty wild position to take. It tastes of raisins, oak and grappa and has alcohol over 15%. I don’t know the pH but it has to be close to 4. Flame off before drinking…

posts.name: J. Migone
posts.cooked:
Jurgen, I’m no pro, don’t claim to be and I did not open this. An expert in this wine opened it. As a guy who has and will continue to buy wines in this price range, I felt that this wine was not worth the money and the La Tour Carnet was much better. We are here to inform and discuss.

While I agree with your thoughts, I believe you have to taste the wine before you buy or buy a bottle to try before committing because if you don’t like the wine now there’s is a high likelihood that you will not like it in 20 years (disregarding how tannic a wine is). There are many wines that are accessible early on and are great for aging, so why spend my money on something I can’t drink wherever I want. Why do people buy without tasting??? Maybe these can be the topics we all discuss in November.

Anyways, drink on.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Jürgen Steinke:

Well – I will not open any good 2010 Bordeaux until the wines hit their 10th Birthday. At a minimum. This is one of the finest and most structured vintages of the last 50 years. The beauty of Bordeaux is the complexity the wines develop with age. Why some people taste a 2010 and a 2012 now and make statements about their inner quality is beyond me. It´s cleat that one will taste tons of fruit as long the wines are not closed and tons of wood when the wines are in their dormant phase. But it´s rare when a Bordeaux shows upfront oak when it is mature – so let´s say 15 or 20 years old. For me and many other Bordeaux lover the wines are of little interest when they are babies or teenagers.

It is interesting to taste a young Bordeaux here and there for science. But one needs tons of talent and experience to make a forecast with substance.

I have a distinct recollection that we’ve had this discussion before, perhaps when I was either going on a tirade about Rolland or how the 2005 modern St Ems are showing.

I cannot disagree with you more in your statement. Of course, both of us are expressing mere opinions.

The direction that many of these modern Chateaux have headed, many under Rolland, are marred by such lavish use of new and toasted oak that the oak does not appear to integrate. The toasted oak note remains a complete “upfront” note and characteristic, especially when the ripe, flabby fruit starts to fade. I’ve had many of these new-fangled wines in that range up to and beyond 15 years, and nothing changes.

Some recent examples:

2002 Pape Clement
2000 Troplong Mondot
2001 Barde Haut
2000 Monbousquet
1998 Monbousquet
2000 Pavie (granted, a much better wine than the above, but oak predominates)
2003 Leoville Poyferre
2004 Leoville Poyferre

As I sit around, I’m sure I can recall some more examples.

I would agree with your premise if you changed the word “rarely” to “generally” and limited it to traditionally-crafted Bordeaux. Rolland, Parker and the modern estates have entered a new realm and have started a new paradigm.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked: What Alfert just said.

posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked:
Jay Miller:

What Alfert just said.

Ditto

I think it is a huge misnomer to say one cannot taste Bordeaux young. You can flame me for this one, but I think producers and critics alike hide behind this theory. With that said, I agree that great Bordeaux -and there are not that many- certainly takes years to shine, decades in some instances. Still, they can be pretty rewarding before this apex, and can be good before ten years. caveat… I don’t buy very much Bordeaux anymore as I saw what was coming down the proverbial pike, vis a vis, this thread et.al.

Jürgen, I think I would rather risk opening a little too early and have a look see, than have ten vintages of shitty wine on which to sit. With the changing styles of so many producers, it is incumbent, in my opinion, to try most of these wines young.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: I had the 2012 Pape Clement next to the 2012 Haut Bailly recently. I found them both very impressive and was surprised not to find a dramatic style difference between the two of them. It wasn’t oak soup. I haven’t liked the way some modern Pape Clements have aged (and things certainly weren’t broke before they modernized), but I didn’t detect any of those issues in this bottle. Both wines had a classic Graves profile. This is a vintage I really like in Graves.
